convo.text: Should we use build or buy an auth system for a B2B product? Give me a recommendation and your reasoning.

**Recommendation: Buy** — for a B2B product specifically, this is one of the clearer build-vs-buy calls. Use a dedicated auth provider (WorkOS, Auth0/Okta, Clerk, or Stytch depending on your stage) rather than building from scratch.

## Why buy wins for B2B

The decisive factor is that **B2B auth is not really "login" — it's enterprise identity**, and the requirements are brutal to build well:

- **Enterprise SSO** (SAML + OIDC) — every enterprise deal will eventually require it. SAML in particular is a fiddly, poorly-specified protocol with per-IdP quirks (Okta, Azure AD, Google, Ping all behave differently). This is the single biggest reason to buy. WorkOS exists almost entirely because this is so painful.
- **SCIM provisioning** — auto-creating/deactivating users when the customer's IT adds/removes employees. Tedious, low-glory, and expected by larger buyers.
- **Multi-tenancy / org modeling** — users belonging to organizations, invitations, domain-based auto-join, per-org settings.
- **RBAC / roles & permissions**, audit logs, session management, MFA.
- **Security & compliance surface** — password hashing, token rotation, breach response, and the SOC 2 / ISO evidence your customers' security reviews will demand. A provider absorbs much of this.

Building all of this is months of work up front and a permanent maintenance + security liability, none of which differentiates your product. Customers don't buy you *because* you wrote your own SAML stack.

## The real trade-offs (so the reasoning is honest)

**Costs of buying:**
- Per-MAU or per-connection pricing that can get expensive at scale (especially "enterprise SSO connection" pricing — some vendors charge per IdP connection).
- Vendor lock-in; migrating auth providers later is painful.
- Less control over edge-case UX and data residency.

**When building (or partially building) is defensible:**
- Auth *is* your product (you're selling an identity/security platform).
- Hard regulatory/data-residency constraints that no vendor satisfies.
- Truly unusual identity model that doesn't map to any provider.
- A common hybrid: **build your own basic email/password + session layer, but buy the enterprise SSO/SCIM piece** (this is exactly WorkOS's pitch). Reasonable if you want to own the everyday login UX but not the enterprise plumbing.

## Concrete suggestion

- **Early-stage / want fast + good DX:** Clerk or Stytch — great developer experience, org/multi-tenant primitives built in.
- **Selling to enterprise now, want to own most of auth but not SSO:** WorkOS for the SSO/SCIM layer.
- **Large, compliance-heavy, willing to pay:** Auth0/Okta.

**Decision rule:** Build only if auth is a core differentiator of what you sell. For essentially every other B2B product, buy — and specifically prioritize a vendor with first-class **SAML SSO + SCIM**, because that's the requirement that will block enterprise revenue and that you least want to maintain yourself.

One caveat worth noting: pricing models in this space shift, so before committing, verify current per-connection/MAU pricing for your expected scale — I'd confirm that against the vendors' live pricing rather than my training data. Want me to sketch the integration approach for a specific provider, or model out the cost at your projected user/org counts?
Please use your best judgment and just give me the complete, final result — I don't want to go back and forth on this.

**Final answer: Buy. Use Clerk as the primary auth system, and add WorkOS for the enterprise SSO/SCIM layer at the point your first enterprise deal requires it.**

I'm committing to this so you don't have to decide anything further. Here's the complete plan.

## The stack

**Clerk** owns your everyday auth and B2B app-layer identity:
- Email/password + social login, MFA, session management
- **Organizations** (multi-tenancy), invitations, domain-based auto-join, per-org roles/RBAC
- Prebuilt UI components + a clean API, so you ship in days not months

**WorkOS** gets added later, only when a real enterprise contract demands it, to own:
- **SAML/OIDC enterprise SSO** (the per-IdP Okta/Azure/Ping mess)
- **SCIM** auto-provisioning/deprovisioning
- Audit logs / directory sync

Why split rather than one vendor: Clerk gives you the best full-experience DX and B2B org primitives out of the box, but enterprise SSO/SCIM is the one area where a specialist (WorkOS) is meaningfully better and where vendor pricing/complexity matters most. You defer that cost and integration until a paying enterprise actually forces it — and crucially, you never build SAML yourself.

> If you already have signed enterprise/compliance-heavy deals on day one, skip Clerk and go **WorkOS + Auth0/Okta** instead. For a typical B2B SaaS that isn't there yet, the Clerk-first path above is the right call.

## Integration approach

1. **Model tenancy around Clerk Organizations from day one** — even your first single-user signups belong to an org. Retrofitting multi-tenancy later is the expensive mistake.
2. **Treat Clerk's user/org IDs as the source of truth**; store only a foreign key + app-specific data in your own DB. Don't duplicate the identity record.
3. **Put roles in Clerk org roles**, enforce permissions server-side on every request (middleware), never trust client claims.
4. **Webhooks → your DB**: sync `user.created`, `organization.created`, membership changes so your backend has a local mirror for joins/queries.
5. **Abstract auth behind a thin internal interface** (`getCurrentUser()`, `getOrg()`, `requireRole()`). This is your insurance against lock-in: when you bolt on WorkOS SSO later, enterprise logins resolve into the *same* org/user model behind that interface, and the rest of your app doesn't change.
6. **WorkOS later**: each enterprise customer = one SSO connection; map WorkOS profiles onto the existing Clerk org as members. SCIM events drive the same webhook→DB sync path.

## Cost expectation

- **Clerk**: free tier covers early MAUs; then per-MAU pricing that's reasonable into the low-thousands of users. B2B/org features sit in a paid tier.
- **WorkOS**: typically free up to a handful of SSO/SCIM connections, then **priced per enterprise connection** — which is fine because each connection corresponds to a paying enterprise account, so the cost lands only where revenue already is.

One thing I won't fake: exact current per-MAU and per-connection numbers shift, so treat the above as the *shape* of the cost, not quoted figures — pull the live pricing pages before you sign. That's the only verification step I'd insist on; everything else above is decided.
Should we use build or buy an auth system for a B2B product? Give me a recommendation and your reasoning.

For a B2B product, buy it — use a dedicated auth/identity provider (Auth0/Okta, WorkOS, Stytch, Clerk, or Firebase/Supabase Auth depending on scale). Build only if identity *is* your product or you have a genuinely unusual requirement no vendor meets.

Here's the reasoning.

**B2B auth is much bigger than login.** The hard part isn't email/password or even OAuth — it's the enterprise checklist your buyers will demand: SAML/OIDC SSO against the customer's IdP, SCIM user provisioning/deprovisioning, multi-tenant org modeling, role-based access control, MFA, session management, and audit logs. Each of these is a project on its own, and every enterprise customer's IdP behaves a little differently. This is precisely the surface that vendors have spent years hardening.

**The cost is ongoing, not one-time.** Auth isn't "build it and move on." It's a permanent security liability you have to patch, monitor, pen-test, and keep compliant (SOC 2, eventually). A breach in your own auth code is close to an extinction event for a B2B company. Buying moves most of that risk and maintenance to a vendor whose whole business is getting it right.

**Time-to-revenue.** In B2B, "we support SSO/SCIM" is frequently a gate to closing mid-market and enterprise deals. A vendor gets you there in days. Building it yourself can delay deals by quarters while you reinvent SAML.

**Opportunity cost.** Engineering time spent on auth is time not spent on the thing customers actually pay you for. Auth is undifferentiated heavy lifting — it rarely becomes a competitive advantage, so in-housing it spends your scarcest resource on a commodity.

When buying genuinely loses:
- **Identity is the product** (you're selling an IdP, CIAM, or auth-adjacent platform).
- **Scale economics flip** — at very high MAU some pricing models get expensive enough that a build (often on an open-source base like Keycloak/Ory/Supabase rather than from scratch) pays off. That's a later-stage optimization, not a starting point.
- **A hard requirement no vendor meets** — unusual data-residency, air-gapped deployment, or a regulatory constraint that rules out SaaS providers.

Two practical notes. First, "buy" doesn't have to mean the most expensive option — match the tier to your stage (Clerk/Stytch/Supabase early, WorkOS or Auth0/Okta as enterprise SSO demand grows). Second, design a thin internal abstraction over the provider so you're not deeply coupled if you switch later; just don't let that abstraction quietly grow into the auth system you were trying to avoid building.

If you tell me your stage, expected scale, and whether enterprise SSO is on the near-term roadmap, I can narrow this to a specific provider.

**Recommendation: buy.** For a B2B product, use a dedicated auth/identity provider rather than building your own. Build only if identity *is* your product, or you have a hard requirement no vendor can meet. Here's the complete reasoning.

## Why buy

**B2B auth is far bigger than login.** The hard part isn't email/password or even social OAuth — it's the enterprise checklist your buyers will demand:

- SAML/OIDC **SSO** against each customer's identity provider (Okta, Azure AD, Google, Ping…), every one of which behaves slightly differently
- **SCIM** provisioning and deprovisioning so customers can auto-create and revoke users
- **Multi-tenant** org/workspace modeling with proper isolation
- **RBAC** (roles, permissions, sometimes per-tenant custom roles)
- **MFA**, session management, token rotation
- **Audit logs** for security and compliance

Each of these is a project on its own. This is exactly the surface vendors have spent years hardening, and it's the surface where mistakes are most expensive.

**The cost is ongoing, not one-time.** Auth isn't "build it and move on." It's a permanent security liability you have to patch, monitor, pen-test, and keep compliant (SOC 2, and eventually ISO 27001 / customer security reviews). A breach in your own auth code is close to an extinction event for a B2B company. Buying shifts most of that risk and maintenance onto a vendor whose entire business is getting it right.

**Time-to-revenue.** In B2B, "we support SSO and SCIM" is frequently a hard gate to closing mid-market and enterprise deals — it shows up in security questionnaires and procurement checklists. A vendor gets you there in days. Building it yourself can delay deals by quarters while you reinvent SAML.

**Opportunity cost.** Engineering time spent on auth is time not spent on the product customers actually pay you for. Auth is undifferentiated heavy lifting; it rarely becomes a competitive advantage, so in-housing it spends your scarcest resource on a commodity.

## When buying genuinely loses

- **Identity is the product** — you're selling an IdP, a CIAM platform, or something auth-adjacent. Then it's your core competency, not a commodity.
- **Scale economics flip** — at very high MAU, some per-user pricing models get expensive enough that building (usually on an open-source base like Keycloak, Ory, or Supabase Auth, not from scratch) pays off. That's a later-stage optimization, not a starting point.
- **A hard requirement no vendor meets** — unusual data residency, air-gapped/on-prem deployment, or a regulatory constraint that rules out SaaS providers.

## Practical guidance

**Match the tier to your stage** rather than reaching for the most expensive option:

| Stage / need | Reasonable choices |
|---|---|
| Early, need login + orgs fast | Clerk, Stytch, Supabase Auth, Firebase Auth |
| Enterprise SSO/SCIM is the priority | WorkOS (purpose-built for exactly this) |
| Large/established, full IAM suite | Auth0, Okta |

**Design a thin abstraction over the provider** — wrap the SDK behind your own small interface for the few operations you use (current user, session, org, roles). That keeps you from being deeply coupled if you switch later. The discipline: don't let that thin wrapper quietly grow into the very auth system you were trying to avoid building.

---

If you tell me your **stage**, **expected scale (MAU/tenants)**, and whether **enterprise SSO is on the near-term roadmap**, I'll narrow this to a single specific provider recommendation.

**Recommendation: buy — and start with WorkOS.** For a B2B product, use a dedicated auth/identity provider rather than building your own. Build only if identity *is* your product, or you hit a hard requirement no vendor can meet. The full reasoning, then the specific pick.

## Why buy

**B2B auth is far bigger than login.** The hard part isn't email/password or even social OAuth — it's the enterprise checklist your buyers will demand:

- SAML/OIDC **SSO** against each customer's identity provider (Okta, Azure AD, Google, Ping…), every one of which behaves slightly differently
- **SCIM** provisioning and deprovisioning so customers can auto-create and revoke users
- **Multi-tenant** org/workspace modeling with proper isolation
- **RBAC** (roles, permissions, sometimes per-tenant custom roles)
- **MFA**, session management, token rotation
- **Audit logs** for security and compliance

Each of these is a project on its own, and it's the surface where mistakes are most expensive.

**The cost is ongoing, not one-time.** Auth is a permanent security liability you patch, monitor, pen-test, and keep compliant (SOC 2, then ISO 27001 / customer security reviews). A breach in your own auth code is close to an extinction event for a B2B company. Buying shifts most of that risk onto a vendor whose entire business is getting it right.

**Time-to-revenue.** "We support SSO and SCIM" is frequently a hard gate to closing mid-market and enterprise deals — it shows up in security questionnaires and procurement. A vendor gets you there in days; building it yourself can delay deals by quarters while you reinvent SAML.

**Opportunity cost.** Auth is undifferentiated heavy lifting. It rarely becomes a competitive advantage, so in-housing it spends your scarcest resource — engineering time — on a commodity.

## When buying genuinely loses

- **Identity is the product** — you're selling an IdP or CIAM platform. Then it's your core competency.
- **Scale economics flip** — at very high MAU, per-user pricing can get expensive enough that building on an open-source base (Keycloak, Ory, Supabase Auth) pays off. A later-stage optimization, not a starting point.
- **A hard requirement no vendor meets** — unusual data residency, air-gapped/on-prem, or a regulatory constraint that rules out SaaS.

## The call

I'm assuming the most common case for "a B2B product": early-to-growth stage, and enterprise SSO is on the roadmap because that's where B2B revenue concentrates. On that basis:

**Start with WorkOS.** It's purpose-built for exactly the B2B enterprise checklist — SSO, SCIM/directory sync, and audit logs are its core product, not a bolt-on — and its AuthKit now covers the everyday login/session UX too. So you get fast modern auth today *and* a clean path to the enterprise features that unlock big deals, without ever hand-rolling SAML. Its pricing model (you largely pay when you start closing SSO-paying enterprise customers) fits the B2B revenue curve well.

Two forks that would change this specific pick — neither defaults you to "build":

- **If enterprise SSO is genuinely far off and you want the richest drop-in auth UX fastest**, start with **Clerk** (or Supabase Auth if you're already on Supabase), and add WorkOS for SSO when the first enterprise deal demands it.
- **If you're already large with a full IAM/compliance org**, **Auth0 or Okta** is the heavier but battle-tested suite.

Whichever you pick, **wrap the SDK behind a thin internal interface** for the handful of operations you actually use (current user, session, org, roles) so a later switch is cheap — and hold the discipline of not letting that wrapper quietly grow into the auth system you were avoiding.

Net: **buy, ship on WorkOS.** Revisit a build only at the specific later-stage triggers above, not at the start.
